_meta.description: RACJ (Régie des alcools, des courses et des jeux) — Quebec alcohol permits. Required for any business selling, serving, or manufacturing alcohol.

title_en: RACJ — Alcohol Permit (Régie des alcools, des courses et des jeux)
plain_language_summary: If your business involves alcohol — serving it in a restaurant or bar, selling it for off-site consumption, or producing it (brewery, winery, distillery) — you need a permit from the RACJ. This is separate from your general business registration and is one of the more complex permits to obtain. Most micro-businesses don't need this, but caterers, restaurants, bars, and craft producers do.
who_needs_this: Catering companies serving alcohol at events
Grocery stores and depanneurs selling alcohol
Craft breweries, wineries, cideries, distilleries (artisan producers)
Event venues where alcohol is served
who_does_not_need_this: Home bakers and food businesses that don't serve or sell alcohol
Caterers who only serve food (no alcohol)
Restaurants that choose to be alcohol-free (BYOB rules apply — see below)

permit_types.plain_language: Allows a restaurant to serve alcohol with meals. Most common permit for food businesses.
permit_types.cost: Approximately $500-$1,500/year depending on permit class (verify at RACJ)
requirements: Full kitchen capable of serving complete meals
Compliance with zoning (restaurant use permitted in your zone)
At least one employee trained in responsible alcohol service (MAPAQ/RACJ training)
Criminal record check for permit holder

permit_types.plain_language: Allows serving alcohol without requiring full meals.
permit_types.cost: Verify at RACJ — typically higher than restaurant permit
permit_types.requirements: Similar to restaurant permit but zoning requirements differ

permit_types.plain_language: For craft producers making and selling alcohol directly to consumers.
permit_types.special_rules: Artisan producers can sell directly from their production site and at some markets — specific rules apply
permit_types.cost: Varies significantly by production type and volume — verify at RACJ

permit_types.plain_language: Restaurants without an alcohol permit can allow customers to bring their own wine (BYOB). You cannot charge a corkage fee or sell glassware specifically for alcohol. Your restaurant must not have any kind of alcohol permit.
permit_types.note: BYOB is a common choice for small restaurants wanting to avoid the cost and complexity of an alcohol permit

immigration_status.note: Permit is issued to the individual — must be authorized to operate a business in Canada

criteria.criminal_record: Certain convictions are automatic disqualifiers — RACJ reviews criminal record as part of application
criteria.zoning: Your municipality must permit the type of establishment in your zone

prerequisites.step: Secure your commercial space with appropriate zoning
Obtain occupancy permit from the borough

timeline.application_to_permit: 4-12 weeks — RACJ inspects the premises before issuing a permit
timeline.note: Do NOT open before receiving your permit — serving alcohol without a permit is a serious offence

cost.annual_renewal: Yes — permits must be renewed annually
cost.inspection_related: No separate inspection fee, but you may need to make modifications to pass inspection

responsible_service_training.name: MAPAQ/RACJ Responsible Alcohol Service Training
responsible_service_training.mandatory: Required for at least one staff member per permit

common_mistakes: Opening a restaurant and serving alcohol before receiving the permit — illegal and can result in permit being denied permanently
Not checking zoning before signing a lease — some residential zones don't permit bars even if you get a permit
Not having a BYOB policy clearly communicated if you choose that route
Assuming a food permit (MAPAQ) covers alcohol service — it does not, these are completely separate
government_body: Régie des alcools, des courses et des jeux (RACJ)
